explanations.1: By definition, the rotation angle Δθ is the ratio of the arc length Δs to the radius of curvature r.
explanations.2: Since 360 degrees equals 2π radians, 180 degrees is exactly half of that, which is π radians.
explanations.3: The linear velocity v is calculated by multiplying the radius r by the angular velocity ω (v = rω).
explanations.4: In a rigid rotating object, every point moves through the same angle in the same amount of time, meaning they share the same angular velocity.
explanations.5: Using Δθ = Δs/r, we divide the arc length of 6.28 m by the radius of 2.0 m to get 3.14 rad.
explanations.6: Centripetal means "center-seeking," and the acceleration in uniform circular motion always points directly toward the center of curvature.
explanations.7: According to the formula a_c = v²/r, centripetal acceleration is proportional to the square of the velocity.
explanations.8: The centripetal acceleration is calculated as v²/r, which is (20 m/s)² / 100 m = 4 m/s².
explanations.9: Using the formula a_c = rω², we multiply 0.10 m by (100 rad/s)² to get 1,000 m/s².
explanations.10: Your body attempts to continue moving in a straight line according to Newton's first law while the car accelerates toward the center of the turn.
explanations.11: On a flat surface, the only horizontal force available to change the car's direction toward the center of the curve is friction between the tires and the road.
explanations.12: The centripetal force F_c = mv²/r, so (1000 kg * (10 m/s)²) / 50 m = 2,000 N.
explanations.13: Ideal banking uses the horizontal component of the normal force to provide the necessary centripetal force, making friction unnecessary at a specific speed.
explanations.14: The formula tan θ = v²/rg shows that the angle is independent of the mass of the object.
explanations.15: At the top of the loop, both the weight of the car and the downward normal force from the track point toward the center, providing the centripetal force.
explanations.16: Centrifugal force is an effect of inertia viewed from a non-inertial (accelerating) frame and has no identifiable physical source.
explanations.17: Due to Earth's counterclockwise rotation (when viewed from above the North Pole), all moving objects in the Northern Hemisphere experience a deflection to the right.
explanations.18: An inertial frame of reference is defined as one where Newton's laws hold true without the need for fictitious forces.
explanations.19: In the Southern Hemisphere, the Coriolis force deflects air to the left, which creates a clockwise circulation around low-pressure centers.
explanations.20: Gravity follows an inverse square law, so increasing distance by a factor of 3 results in a force of 1/3², or 1/9 of the original.
explanations.21: Plugging these values into F = GmM/r² yields the value of the gravitational constant G.
explanations.22: Since g = GM/r², doubling mass (x2) and doubling radius (x4 in the denominator) results in 2/4 = 0.5 times the original g.
explanations.23: Newton's third law states that for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force, regardless of the masses involved.
explanations.24: Tides are caused by the gradient of the Moon's gravitational pull, which is stronger on the side of Earth facing the Moon and weaker on the far side.
explanations.25: This law describes the observation that planets move faster when they are closer to the Sun and slower when they are further away.
explanations.26: According to Kepler's Third Law (T² ∝ r³), if r increases by a factor of 4, T² increases by 4³ (64), so T increases by the square root of 64, which is 8.
explanations.27: In the derivation of orbital speed (v = √(GM/r)), the mass of the satellite cancels out, meaning only the mass of the parent body matters.
explanations.28: The Copernican or heliocentric model correctly identifies the Sun as the center of the planetary orbits, a view later proven by Newton's laws.
